romania.title: Cristi Mungiu explică „Fjord”, filmul cu care a luat Palme d’Or la Cannes: „N-am făcut filmul în Belarus, ci în Norvegia”
romania.summary: „Acest film este un angajament împotriva oricărui tip de fundamentalism”, a spus regizorul român care a câștigat pentru a doua oară Palme d’Or. „Nu este o diferență atât de mare între fundamentalismul de dreapta și cel de stânga”, crede Mungiu. „Fjord” a lui Cristian Mungiu este un film despre o familie de români care emigrează …

romania.summary: Bătrânul Tolea Ciumac, fost luptător de K-1, invitat în platou la Dan Diaconescu la emisiuni de circ și bodyguard, a decis să-și facă un rebranding personal și a schimbat numele în Tolea Ebola.  Noul nume este mult mai actual, pentru că Ebola a început să facă din nou victime în Africa și îngrozește populația, în […] The post Modernizare! Tolea Ciumac și-a schimbat numele în Tolea Ebola appeared first on Times New Roman .

Savanții au botezat noua pasăre Papagalus Călinus Georgescus, în onoarea celui mai puternic politician român al momentului. Specia Călinus Georgescus poate vorbi fără întrerupere până la șase ore și învață automat texte de pagini întregi, pe care le redă cu […] The post Savanții au identificat o nouă specie de papagal vorbitor și l-au numit Călinus Georgescus appeared first on Times New Roman .

Procesul este unul de durată, dar la final se estimează că un dosar de corupție se va prescrie în maxim două ore de la data comiterii faptei, iar corupții nu vor mai […] The post Statul român alocă un miliard de euro pentru cercetare în domeniul accelerării prescripției appeared first on Times New Roman .

medical.title: Weight loss drug semaglutide helps patients who do not respond to bariatric surgery, research finds
medical.summary: Metabolic/bariatric surgery is a highly effective treatment for people living with severe obesity and/or metabolic health conditions, which works through changing the anatomy of the digestive system and thereby changing the way it functions and the hormones it produces. Procedures include the sleeve gastrectomy and gastric bypass.

medical.title: New AI tool may help personalize multiple myeloma treatment
medical.summary: An artificial intelligence-based tool may help physicians determine which newly diagnosed multiple myeloma patients are most likely to benefit from specific therapies, including immunotherapy and stem cell transplantation.

medical.title: Ovarian cancer cells use stress hormone signaling to shut down immune system, research reveals
medical.summary: When activated in ovarian cancer cells, the receptor for the body's primary stress hormone alters the tumor environment in ways that blunt immune response, according to new research led by UT Southwestern Medical Center. The findings, published in Endocrinology, identify a previously unrecognized role for the glucocorticoid receptor (GR) in shaping the ovarian cancer tumor microenvironment.

medical.title: Advocacy is key to preserving vital vaccine research, researchers say
medical.summary: Scientists and physicians should advocate to protect the vaccine research infrastructure that has saved an estimated 154 million lives over the past 50 years, according to a new commentary by researchers at Weill Cornell Medicine and the University of Washington.

medical.title: Study reveals brain changes linked to alcohol addiction recovery
medical.summary: Scientists say they've uncovered striking new evidence of how alcohol addiction impacts the brain's learning systems—and how those systems may slowly adapt during recovery—in a new study published in Clinical Neurophysiology today, May 22.

medical.title: A single dose of psilocybin eased depression symptoms for months, our study found
medical.summary: A single dose of psilocybin eased symptoms of depression within days, with benefits lasting for more than three months compared to placebo, our new study has found.

medical.title: Key gene boundary discovery may improve treatment of rare inflammatory disorder
medical.summary: Not all broken genes fail in the same way: some simply stop working, while others interfere with what still works. Researchers from Hiroshima University have identified a critical boundary within the immune-regulating gene called RELA that helps predict how harmful mutations cause disease. Their findings could improve diagnosis and treatment for patients with a rare inherited inflammatory disease.

medical.title: Surprising study finds beef doesn’t worsen blood sugar or diabetes risk
medical.summary: A new clinical trial suggests that eating beef every day may not be as risky for people with prediabetes as many assume. Researchers found that adults who ate 6–7 ounces of beef daily for a month showed no worsening in blood sugar control, insulin function, inflammation, or other key markers linked to type 2 diabetes when compared to people eating poultry instead.

medical.title: Support for adolescents in military families can improve their mental health
medical.summary: Youth in military families need support from parents and peers to maintain a healthy mental well-being, according to a new study from the University of Georgia. Researchers found these relationships are connected to adolescents' abilities to utilize adaptive coping skills, such as problem-solving and self-reliance, which in turn promote their well-being.

medical.title: Cancer, mood, anxiety, sleep: It's all connected, and yoga eases symptoms for survivors
medical.summary: University of Rochester Medicine is in the spotlight at a national meeting for a new discovery in integrative oncology: A special yoga intervention goes a step further than other therapies to improve four domains of physical and psychological side effects that most cancer patients experience, including lifting mood and easing anxiety.

medical.title: Mental health difficulties among young adults have doubled in past decade
medical.summary: More than a fifth (22%) of generation Z in England report having a longstanding mental health condition in their early 20s, double the rate of millennials (10%) at a similar age 10 years earlier, finds a new UCL study.

medical.title: Scientists uncover surprising health benefits of watermelon
medical.summary: Studies suggest watermelon could be a hidden powerhouse for better health. Researchers found that people who eat watermelon tend to have higher-quality diets packed with more vitamins, fiber, and antioxidants — while consuming less added sugar and saturated fat. Another study showed watermelon juice may help protect blood vessel function and support heart health.

medical.title: Scientists reveal how seven days of fasting transforms the human body
medical.summary: Scientists have discovered that the human body undergoes a dramatic internal transformation during extended fasting, with major changes appearing only after about three days without food. In a seven-day water-only fasting study, researchers tracked thousands of proteins in the blood and found widespread shifts affecting organs throughout the body — including the brain. While the body quickly switches from burning glucose to fat, the most intriguing biological changes linked to potential health benefits didn’t emerge until later in the fast.

medical.title: Scientists reverse Alzheimer’s in mice with breakthrough nanotechnology
medical.summary: A new nanotechnology treatment reversed Alzheimer’s symptoms in mice by restoring the brain’s natural cleanup system. The specially engineered nanoparticles helped clear toxic amyloid proteins from the brain and repair the blood-brain barrier, which normally protects and regulates the brain’s environment. In one striking experiment, elderly mice treated with the therapy later behaved like healthy younger mice.

medical.title: Scientists discover hidden fat-burning switch that could strengthen bones
medical.summary: Scientists at McGill University have uncovered a hidden molecular “switch” that turns on a powerful calorie-burning system in brown fat — the body’s heat-generating fat linked to metabolism and weight control. The breakthrough centers on glycerol, a molecule released when fat is broken down in the cold, which activates an enzyme called TNAP and triggers an alternative heat-producing pathway that scientists had struggled to explain for years.

medical.title: ‘It’s so full of love’, the festival for young carers
medical.summary: For one weekend a year, the Young Carers Festival helps them swap adult responsibilities for the simple joy of being a child The post ‘It’s so full of love’, the festival for young carers appeared first on Positive News .

science.title: Some technologies use accelerated natural processes to capture carbon, but can they store it durably?
science.summary: Natural geological processes have been regulating Earth's climate for millions of years. Accelerated versions of these processes are now being promoted as technologies to draw down carbon from the atmosphere—and some are rapidly moving from concept to real-world deployments.

science.title: Arctic thaw unleashes mining-like pollution across hundreds Arctic waterways
science.summary: Thawing permafrost is rapidly transforming dozens of Arctic streams into acidic, metal-laden waterways, according to new research published in Science. The study shows how thawing permafrost exposes sulfide minerals that react with oxygen and water—a process similar to what occurs in mining pollution. The reactions release acidity and heavy metals such as zinc, nickel, cadmium, and aluminum into surrounding waters.

science.title: NASA Sets Coverage for Roscosmos Spacewalk Outside Space Station
science.summary: NASA will provide live coverage on Wednesday, May 27, as two Roscosmos cosmonauts conduct a spacewalk outside the International Space Station. The spacewalk is scheduled to begin at approximately 10:15 a.m. EDT and last roughly five hours. Watch NASA’s live coverage beginning at 9:45 a.m. on NASA+, Amazon Prime, and the agency’s YouTube channel. Learn […]

science.title: Physicists figure out how to reduce formation of 'viscous fingers'
science.summary: When they reach the bottom of a soap dispenser, frugal handwashers might try adding water to the bottle to push out the last bit of soap. But usually, the water drills right through the soap and jets out an only slightly sudsy splash.

science.title: Student talent drives simpler method for programming artificial muscles in soft robots
science.summary: An interdisciplinary student research team at the University of Waterloo has achieved an advance in materials science with the creation of a tissue-like hydrogel for artificial muscles to make soft robots move.

science.title: Scientists discover thriving hard-substrate fauna in Oceania's deep sea
science.summary: In the crushing darkness of the hadal zone—deep ocean trenches plunging 6,000 m to nearly 11,000 m—scientists have uncovered a hidden community. A study published in Science on May 14 reports the discovery of a protist-dominated hard-substrate fauna across seven hadal regions in Oceania, highlighting an overlooked yet highly active carbon "hotspot."

science.title: 'Designer' superconducting diamond: Researchers uncover path to multi-modality quantum chips
science.summary: Diamond is extremely valuable to science and technology not for its sparkle but for its extreme hardness, high thermal conductivity, transparency to a large fraction of the light spectrum, and a host of other exceptional properties. Two decades ago, scientists discovered another advantage: under the right conditions, diamond can become a superconductor—allowing electricity to flow through it with zero resistance.

science.title: South China Sea coral reefs reveal carbon stores rivaling mangroves and seagrasses
science.summary: A collaborative research team has revealed the long-overlooked carbon storage potential of coral reef ecosystems and how reef-dwelling fish, corals, and surface sediments jointly shape reef carbon reservoirs. The paper is published in the journal Advanced Science. The team was led by The Hong Kong University of Science and Technology (HKUST), the Institute of Zoology at the Chinese Academy of Sciences (IOZ-CAS) and the Southern Marine Science and Engineering Guangdong Laboratory (Guangzhou) (GML).

science.title: SpaceX to retry Starship test launch Friday
science.summary: Elon Musk's SpaceX will attempt another launch of its mammoth Starship rocket Friday, after the previous evening's try was called off over technical issues.

environment.title: This 2-pound dinosaur is rewriting what scientists know about evolution
environment.summary: A nearly complete dinosaur skeleton discovered in Patagonia is helping scientists crack the mystery of alvarezsaurs, a bizarre group of bird-like dinosaurs. The fossil of Alnashetri cerropoliciensis reveals that these animals became tiny before developing their later specialized features, such as stubby arms and ant-eating adaptations. Weighing under two pounds, the dinosaur is one of the smallest known from South America.

environment.title: Scientists finally solve the mystery of yeast’s tiny centromeres
environment.summary: Scientists have uncovered how brewer’s yeast developed its unusually tiny centromeres, the DNA regions that guide chromosome separation during cell division. By studying related yeast species, researchers found centromeres that appear to represent evolutionary halfway points. These structures seem to have formed from retrotransposons—mobile “jumping genes” in the genome. The discovery shows how DNA once considered genomic junk can be transformed into essential chromosome machinery.

environment.title: Light-guided evolution creates proteins that can switch, sense, and compute
environment.summary: Researchers have created a method called optovolution that uses light to guide the evolution of proteins with dynamic behaviors. By engineering yeast cells so their survival depended on proteins switching states at the right time, scientists could rapidly select the best-performing variants. The technique produced new light-sensitive proteins that respond to different colors and improved optogenetic systems. It even evolved a protein that behaves like a tiny logic gate, activating genes only when two signals are present.

environment.title: Scientists discover hidden species among Borneo’s “fanged frogs”
environment.summary: DNA is revealing that many animals once thought to be a single species may actually be several hidden ones. But research on Bornean fanged frogs shows the line between species can be blurry—an important challenge when deciding what wildlife needs protection most.

environment.title: Lake Coatepeque
environment.summary: Set amid El Salvador’s modern, active volcanic landscape, tranquil blue waters fill a caldera formed by ancient eruptions. The post Lake Coatepeque appeared first on NASA Science .

environment.title: Scientists stunned to find signs of ancient life in a place no one expected
environment.summary: While exploring ancient seabeds in Morocco, scientists discovered strange wrinkle-like textures in deep-water sediments that shouldn’t have been there. These structures are usually made by sunlight-loving microbial mats in shallow waters. But the rocks formed far below the reach of light, suggesting a different explanation. Evidence points to chemosynthetic microbes—organisms powered by chemical reactions—creating the mats in the dark depths of an ancient ocean.

environment.title: Scientists finally reveal why mint feels cold
environment.summary: Scientists have revealed how the body’s microscopic cold sensor, TRPM8, detects both chilly temperatures and the cooling effect of menthol. The discovery finally shows how the sensation of “cool” works at the molecular level—and could inspire new treatments for pain and eye disorders.

environment.title: Bird droppings helped build one of ancient Peru’s most powerful kingdoms
environment.summary: New research suggests seabird guano helped transform the Chincha Kingdom into one of the most prosperous societies in ancient Peru. Chemical clues in centuries-old maize show farmers fertilized their crops with guano gathered from nearby islands, dramatically boosting yields in the desert landscape. The resulting agricultural surplus fueled trade, population growth, and regional influence.

mix_items.title: Cristi Mungiu explică „Fjord”, filmul cu care a luat Palme d’Or la Cannes: „N-am făcut filmul în Belarus, ci în Norvegia”
mix_items.summary: „Acest film este un angajament împotriva oricărui tip de fundamentalism”, a spus regizorul român care a câștigat pentru a doua oară Palme d’Or. „Nu este o diferență atât de mare între fundamentalismul de dreapta și cel de stânga”, crede Mungiu. „Fjord” a lui Cristian Mungiu este un film despre o familie de români care emigrează …

mix_items.title: Festivalul Namaste India revine cu ediția a 17-a, la Grădina Botanică din București

mix_items.summary: Bătrânul Tolea Ciumac, fost luptător de K-1, invitat în platou la Dan Diaconescu la emisiuni de circ și bodyguard, a decis să-și facă un rebranding personal și a schimbat numele în Tolea Ebola.  Noul nume este mult mai actual, pentru că Ebola a început să facă din nou victime în Africa și îngrozește populația, în […] The post Modernizare! Tolea Ciumac și-a schimbat numele în Tolea Ebola appeared first on Times New Roman .

mix_items.title: 😂 Savanții au identificat o nouă specie de papagal vorbitor și l-au numit Călinus Georgescus
mix_items.summary: Descoperire semnificativă în Amazon, unde savanții români au identificat o specie nouă de papagal vorbitor. Savanții au botezat noua pasăre Papagalus Călinus Georgescus, în onoarea celui mai puternic politician român al momentului. Specia Călinus Georgescus poate vorbi fără întrerupere până la șase ore și învață automat texte de pagini întregi, pe care le redă cu […] The post Savanții au identificat o nouă specie de papagal vorbitor și l-au numit Călinus Georgescus appeared first on Times New Roman .

mix_items.summary: Cine a zis că în România nu se face cercetare? Ministerul Justiției alocă un miliard de euro pentru cercetări în domeniul accelerării prescripției. Procesul este unul de durată, dar la final se estimează că un dosar de corupție se va prescrie în maxim două ore de la data comiterii faptei, iar corupții nu vor mai […] The post Statul român alocă un miliard de euro pentru cercetare în domeniul accelerării prescripției appeared first on Times New Roman .

mix_items.title: Some technologies use accelerated natural processes to capture carbon, but can they store it durably?
mix_items.summary: Natural geological processes have been regulating Earth's climate for millions of years. Accelerated versions of these processes are now being promoted as technologies to draw down carbon from the atmosphere—and some are rapidly moving from concept to real-world deployments.

mix_items.title: Arctic thaw unleashes mining-like pollution across hundreds Arctic waterways
mix_items.summary: Thawing permafrost is rapidly transforming dozens of Arctic streams into acidic, metal-laden waterways, according to new research published in Science. The study shows how thawing permafrost exposes sulfide minerals that react with oxygen and water—a process similar to what occurs in mining pollution. The reactions release acidity and heavy metals such as zinc, nickel, cadmium, and aluminum into surrounding waters.

mix_items.summary: NASA will provide live coverage on Wednesday, May 27, as two Roscosmos cosmonauts conduct a spacewalk outside the International Space Station. The spacewalk is scheduled to begin at approximately 10:15 a.m. EDT and last roughly five hours. Watch NASA’s live coverage beginning at 9:45 a.m. on NASA+, Amazon Prime, and the agency’s YouTube channel. Learn […]

mix_items.title: Physicists figure out how to reduce formation of 'viscous fingers'
mix_items.summary: When they reach the bottom of a soap dispenser, frugal handwashers might try adding water to the bottle to push out the last bit of soap. But usually, the water drills right through the soap and jets out an only slightly sudsy splash.

mix_items.title: Angajăm Asistent Social pentru Telefonul Vârstnicului la București!
mix_items.summary: Cine suntem noi și de cine avem nevoie? Suntem echipa de la Telefonul Vârstnicului, serviciu social licențiat, singura linie telefonică gratuită și confidențială, accesibilă la nivel național, dedicată persoanelor vârstnice, ce oferă consiliere socială și alinarea singurătății. În fiecare an suntem sunați de peste 5,000... Post-ul Angajăm Asistent Social pentru Telefonul Vârstnicului la București! apare prima dată în Fundația Regală Margareta a României .

mix_items.summary: Un drum construit prin educație Simona este elevă în clasa a XI-a, la specializarea Tehnician în industria alimentară, la un... Articolul Simona, eleva care descoperă viitorul industriei alimentare pas cu pas apare prima dată în World Vision România .

mix_items.title: Student talent drives simpler method for programming artificial muscles in soft robots
mix_items.summary: An interdisciplinary student research team at the University of Waterloo has achieved an advance in materials science with the creation of a tissue-like hydrogel for artificial muscles to make soft robots move.

mix_items.title: Psiholog
mix_items.summary: Locație: București | Nivel carieră: Experiență de minimum 1 an Candidatul Ideal: Ești tu vocea de care au nevoie? Un copil are nevoie de iubire, siguranță și încredere pentru a... The post Psiholog appeared first on SOS Satele Copiilor România .

mix_items.title: Scientists discover thriving hard-substrate fauna in Oceania's deep sea
mix_items.summary: In the crushing darkness of the hadal zone—deep ocean trenches plunging 6,000 m to nearly 11,000 m—scientists have uncovered a hidden community. A study published in Science on May 14 reports the discovery of a protist-dominated hard-substrate fauna across seven hadal regions in Oceania, highlighting an overlooked yet highly active carbon "hotspot."

mix_items.title: 'Designer' superconducting diamond: Researchers uncover path to multi-modality quantum chips
mix_items.summary: Diamond is extremely valuable to science and technology not for its sparkle but for its extreme hardness, high thermal conductivity, transparency to a large fraction of the light spectrum, and a host of other exceptional properties. Two decades ago, scientists discovered another advantage: under the right conditions, diamond can become a superconductor—allowing electricity to flow through it with zero resistance.

mix_items.title: South China Sea coral reefs reveal carbon stores rivaling mangroves and seagrasses
mix_items.summary: A collaborative research team has revealed the long-overlooked carbon storage potential of coral reef ecosystems and how reef-dwelling fish, corals, and surface sediments jointly shape reef carbon reservoirs. The paper is published in the journal Advanced Science. The team was led by The Hong Kong University of Science and Technology (HKUST), the Institute of Zoology at the Chinese Academy of Sciences (IOZ-CAS) and the Southern Marine Science and Engineering Guangdong Laboratory (Guangzhou) (GML).

mix_items.title: SpaceX to retry Starship test launch Friday
mix_items.summary: Elon Musk's SpaceX will attempt another launch of its mammoth Starship rocket Friday, after the previous evening's try was called off over technical issues.

mix_items.title: Weight loss drug semaglutide helps patients who do not respond to bariatric surgery, research finds
mix_items.summary: Metabolic/bariatric surgery is a highly effective treatment for people living with severe obesity and/or metabolic health conditions, which works through changing the anatomy of the digestive system and thereby changing the way it functions and the hormones it produces. Procedures include the sleeve gastrectomy and gastric bypass.

mix_items.title: New AI tool may help personalize multiple myeloma treatment
mix_items.summary: An artificial intelligence-based tool may help physicians determine which newly diagnosed multiple myeloma patients are most likely to benefit from specific therapies, including immunotherapy and stem cell transplantation.

mix_items.title: Ovarian cancer cells use stress hormone signaling to shut down immune system, research reveals
mix_items.summary: When activated in ovarian cancer cells, the receptor for the body's primary stress hormone alters the tumor environment in ways that blunt immune response, according to new research led by UT Southwestern Medical Center. The findings, published in Endocrinology, identify a previously unrecognized role for the glucocorticoid receptor (GR) in shaping the ovarian cancer tumor microenvironment.

mix_items.title: Advocacy is key to preserving vital vaccine research, researchers say
mix_items.summary: Scientists and physicians should advocate to protect the vaccine research infrastructure that has saved an estimated 154 million lives over the past 50 years, according to a new commentary by researchers at Weill Cornell Medicine and the University of Washington.

mix_items.title: Study reveals brain changes linked to alcohol addiction recovery
mix_items.summary: Scientists say they've uncovered striking new evidence of how alcohol addiction impacts the brain's learning systems—and how those systems may slowly adapt during recovery—in a new study published in Clinical Neurophysiology today, May 22.

mix_items.title: A single dose of psilocybin eased depression symptoms for months, our study found
mix_items.summary: A single dose of psilocybin eased symptoms of depression within days, with benefits lasting for more than three months compared to placebo, our new study has found.

mix_items.title: Key gene boundary discovery may improve treatment of rare inflammatory disorder
mix_items.summary: Not all broken genes fail in the same way: some simply stop working, while others interfere with what still works. Researchers from Hiroshima University have identified a critical boundary within the immune-regulating gene called RELA that helps predict how harmful mutations cause disease. Their findings could improve diagnosis and treatment for patients with a rare inherited inflammatory disease.

mix_items.title: Surprising study finds beef doesn’t worsen blood sugar or diabetes risk
mix_items.summary: A new clinical trial suggests that eating beef every day may not be as risky for people with prediabetes as many assume. Researchers found that adults who ate 6–7 ounces of beef daily for a month showed no worsening in blood sugar control, insulin function, inflammation, or other key markers linked to type 2 diabetes when compared to people eating poultry instead.

mix_items.title: Support for adolescents in military families can improve their mental health
mix_items.summary: Youth in military families need support from parents and peers to maintain a healthy mental well-being, according to a new study from the University of Georgia. Researchers found these relationships are connected to adolescents' abilities to utilize adaptive coping skills, such as problem-solving and self-reliance, which in turn promote their well-being.

mix_items.title: Cancer, mood, anxiety, sleep: It's all connected, and yoga eases symptoms for survivors
mix_items.summary: University of Rochester Medicine is in the spotlight at a national meeting for a new discovery in integrative oncology: A special yoga intervention goes a step further than other therapies to improve four domains of physical and psychological side effects that most cancer patients experience, including lifting mood and easing anxiety.

mix_items.title: Mental health difficulties among young adults have doubled in past decade
mix_items.summary: More than a fifth (22%) of generation Z in England report having a longstanding mental health condition in their early 20s, double the rate of millennials (10%) at a similar age 10 years earlier, finds a new UCL study.

mix_items.title: Scientists uncover surprising health benefits of watermelon
mix_items.summary: Studies suggest watermelon could be a hidden powerhouse for better health. Researchers found that people who eat watermelon tend to have higher-quality diets packed with more vitamins, fiber, and antioxidants — while consuming less added sugar and saturated fat. Another study showed watermelon juice may help protect blood vessel function and support heart health.

mix_items.title: Scientists reveal how seven days of fasting transforms the human body
mix_items.summary: Scientists have discovered that the human body undergoes a dramatic internal transformation during extended fasting, with major changes appearing only after about three days without food. In a seven-day water-only fasting study, researchers tracked thousands of proteins in the blood and found widespread shifts affecting organs throughout the body — including the brain. While the body quickly switches from burning glucose to fat, the most intriguing biological changes linked to potential health benefits didn’t emerge until later in the fast.

mix_items.title: Scientists reverse Alzheimer’s in mice with breakthrough nanotechnology
mix_items.summary: A new nanotechnology treatment reversed Alzheimer’s symptoms in mice by restoring the brain’s natural cleanup system. The specially engineered nanoparticles helped clear toxic amyloid proteins from the brain and repair the blood-brain barrier, which normally protects and regulates the brain’s environment. In one striking experiment, elderly mice treated with the therapy later behaved like healthy younger mice.

mix_items.title: Scientists discover hidden fat-burning switch that could strengthen bones
mix_items.summary: Scientists at McGill University have uncovered a hidden molecular “switch” that turns on a powerful calorie-burning system in brown fat — the body’s heat-generating fat linked to metabolism and weight control. The breakthrough centers on glycerol, a molecule released when fat is broken down in the cold, which activates an enzyme called TNAP and triggers an alternative heat-producing pathway that scientists had struggled to explain for years.

mix_items.title: ‘It’s so full of love’, the festival for young carers
mix_items.summary: For one weekend a year, the Young Carers Festival helps them swap adult responsibilities for the simple joy of being a child The post ‘It’s so full of love’, the festival for young carers appeared first on Positive News .

mix_items.title: This 2-pound dinosaur is rewriting what scientists know about evolution
mix_items.summary: A nearly complete dinosaur skeleton discovered in Patagonia is helping scientists crack the mystery of alvarezsaurs, a bizarre group of bird-like dinosaurs. The fossil of Alnashetri cerropoliciensis reveals that these animals became tiny before developing their later specialized features, such as stubby arms and ant-eating adaptations. Weighing under two pounds, the dinosaur is one of the smallest known from South America.

mix_items.title: Scientists finally solve the mystery of yeast’s tiny centromeres
mix_items.summary: Scientists have uncovered how brewer’s yeast developed its unusually tiny centromeres, the DNA regions that guide chromosome separation during cell division. By studying related yeast species, researchers found centromeres that appear to represent evolutionary halfway points. These structures seem to have formed from retrotransposons—mobile “jumping genes” in the genome. The discovery shows how DNA once considered genomic junk can be transformed into essential chromosome machinery.

mix_items.title: Light-guided evolution creates proteins that can switch, sense, and compute
mix_items.summary: Researchers have created a method called optovolution that uses light to guide the evolution of proteins with dynamic behaviors. By engineering yeast cells so their survival depended on proteins switching states at the right time, scientists could rapidly select the best-performing variants. The technique produced new light-sensitive proteins that respond to different colors and improved optogenetic systems. It even evolved a protein that behaves like a tiny logic gate, activating genes only when two signals are present.

mix_items.title: Scientists discover hidden species among Borneo’s “fanged frogs”
mix_items.summary: DNA is revealing that many animals once thought to be a single species may actually be several hidden ones. But research on Bornean fanged frogs shows the line between species can be blurry—an important challenge when deciding what wildlife needs protection most.

mix_items.title: Lake Coatepeque
mix_items.summary: Set amid El Salvador’s modern, active volcanic landscape, tranquil blue waters fill a caldera formed by ancient eruptions. The post Lake Coatepeque appeared first on NASA Science .

mix_items.title: Scientists stunned to find signs of ancient life in a place no one expected
mix_items.summary: While exploring ancient seabeds in Morocco, scientists discovered strange wrinkle-like textures in deep-water sediments that shouldn’t have been there. These structures are usually made by sunlight-loving microbial mats in shallow waters. But the rocks formed far below the reach of light, suggesting a different explanation. Evidence points to chemosynthetic microbes—organisms powered by chemical reactions—creating the mats in the dark depths of an ancient ocean.

mix_items.title: Scientists finally reveal why mint feels cold
mix_items.summary: Scientists have revealed how the body’s microscopic cold sensor, TRPM8, detects both chilly temperatures and the cooling effect of menthol. The discovery finally shows how the sensation of “cool” works at the molecular level—and could inspire new treatments for pain and eye disorders.

mix_items.title: Bird droppings helped build one of ancient Peru’s most powerful kingdoms
mix_items.summary: New research suggests seabird guano helped transform the Chincha Kingdom into one of the most prosperous societies in ancient Peru. Chemical clues in centuries-old maize show farmers fertilized their crops with guano gathered from nearby islands, dramatically boosting yields in the desert landscape. The resulting agricultural surplus fueled trade, population growth, and regional influence.

joke_ro.text: Ce faci diseara? - Ma odihnesc de la cat n-am facut azi.

satire_ro.title: Modernizare! Tolea Ciumac și-a schimbat numele în Tolea Ebola
